name: Ran
description: Ran is the Norse goddess of the sea, specifically associated with the depths and the dangers they hold. She is the wife of Ægir, the personification of the ocean, and together they dwell in a magnificent hall beneath the waves, often described as being illuminated by gold. Ran is primarily known for her net, which she uses to capture sailors and drag them down to her watery realm. These drowned sailors are not necessarily seen as victims, but rather as honored guests in Ran's hall, where they are treated with respect and receive a hero's welcome. Some accounts suggest that Ran collects the souls of those who drown, while others portray her as a more benevolent figure who provides safe passage to those who appease her. Her character is complex, embodying both the allure and the peril of the sea. She is often depicted as greedy, desiring the gold of shipwrecks, but also as a powerful force of nature to be respected. Ran's influence extends to all aspects of seafaring, from trade and exploration to warfare and fishing. Understanding Ran's power and providing her with offerings was crucial for ensuring a safe voyage.
subtitle: Goddess of the Sea's Depths

keyMyths.title: Ran's Net
keyMyths.description: Ran uses her net to capture sailors and drag them to her underwater hall. This net is a symbol of her power over the sea.

keyMyths.title: Ran and Ægir's Feast
keyMyths.description: Ran and Ægir host lavish feasts for the gods in their underwater hall, lit by shimmering gold. These feasts showcase their wealth and influence.

keyMyths.title: Offerings to Ran
keyMyths.description: Sailors would offer gold and other valuables to Ran to ensure a safe voyage and avoid being caught in her net. These offerings were a sign of respect.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: A primary source for Norse mythology, including descriptions of Ran.
sources.text: A primary source for Norse mythology, including descriptions of Ran.
